data.title: Sector Update: Consumer Stocks Decline in Late Afternoon Trading
data.description:
Consumer stocks were sliding late Thursday afternoon, with the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLP) down 0.5% and the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY) falling 1%.

In corporate news, Hasbro shares jumped past 13% after it reported a surprise increase in Q4 profit and outlined a medium-term strategy, including targeting $1 billion of cost savings.

Dollar Tree's discount retail brand Family Dollar has attracted takeover interest from several private equity firms including Apollo Global Management (APO) and Sycamore Partners, Reuters reported. Dollar Tree shares added 0.8%.

Capri , the parent company of Versace and Michael Kors, was downgraded two notches to BB from BBB- by S&P Global Ratings to reflect the company's "weak" operating performance over the past two years and expectations of negative sales trends in the coming year, S&P Global Ratings said Thursday. Capri shares fell 3%.

Walmart issued a full-year earnings outlook below market estimates amid uncertain consumer behavior and geopolitical conditions after reporting better-than-expected fiscal Q4 results. The shares dropped 6.5%.

data.title: Nanosonics Price Target Raised 23% to A$4.55/Share by Barrenjoey
Summit Ridge Energy Announces Promotion of Matt Vitorla to General Counsel
data.description:
Arlington, VA, Feb. 20, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Summit Ridge Energy, the nation’s leading commercial solar company, is proud to announce the promotion of Matt Vitorla to the position of General Counsel. In this new role, Vitorla will oversee all legal and compliance matters for the company as it continues its mission to power America's energy independence. 

Vitorla joined Summit Ridge in 2023 as Associate General Counsel, bringing with him a wealth of legal expertise and a deep understanding of the renewable energy sector from his experience in private practice. During his tenure, he has been instrumental in managing complex corporate transactions, supporting the company’s growth initiatives and ensuring regulatory compliance.  

"Matt’s promotion reflects his exceptional contributions to Summit Ridge Energy and his commitment to our vision of a sustainable, energy-independent future," said Steve Raeder, Chairman & CEO of Summit Ridge Energy. "We are confident that his leadership as General Counsel will further strengthen our ability to navigate the evolving legal landscape of the renewable energy industry." 

 “I’m grateful for the opportunity to serve as General Counsel at Summit Ridge Energy and to continue working alongside and advising such a talented team,” said Vitorla. “As the company grows, ensuring strong legal and regulatory foundations will be critical to our success. I look forward to supporting our efforts to expand access to solar energy and strengthen America’s energy independence.” 

Vitorla holds a J.D. from the University of Pennsylvania Law School and has been engaged in the energy and project finance fields for over eight years. His expertise will be critical as Summit Ridge continues to lead the way in solar development and innovation.  

About Summit Ridge Energy  

As the nation’s leading commercial solar company, Summit Ridge Energy merges financial innovation and industry-leading execution to deliver locally generated energy via a more resilient and secure electric grid. This has made Summit Ridge one of the fastest-growing energy companies in America, with over 2GW of solar power operating and in development.  

Since launching in 2017, Summit Ridge has raised over $5B in project capital to finance more than 200 solar farms, providing energy savings to more than 40,000 homes and businesses while contributing to American energy independence. Learn more at srenergy.com and connect with us on LinkedIn.   

 ###   

Attachment

  • Matt Vitorla, General Counsel at Summit Ridge Energy

Antonya Asante

Summit Ridge Energy

347-723-7231

press@srenergy.com

Business Development

business@srenergy.com

data.title: AIC Mines Price Target Cut 3.2% to A$0.61/Share by Ord Minnett
Coffee falls more than 5% amid pause in roaster buying, profit taking
data.description:
Arabica coffee futures on the ICE exchange fell sharply on Thursday as roasters paused their buying spree, having stocked up in recent weeks and amid some profit-taking by speculators, while raw sugar hit a fresh two-month peak.

COFFEE

* Arabica coffee settled down 22 cents, or 5.3%, at $3.899 per lb, edging away from last week's record high of $4.2995.

* Dealers said data indicates roasters had likely stocked up for now, following strong purchases in recent weeks after giving up hope of a drop in prices.

* Bullish speculators are meanwhile taking profit or selling every time the market rises so that they do not have too much money invested in coffee, the dealers added.

* "I believe funds would have cut around 6,000 contracts from their long position," said Vicente Zotti at broker Pine Agronegocios.

* Still, arabica coffee cannot fall much in the near term given the prospect of a smaller 2025/26 crop in top grower Brazil. The crop was projected 8% down on Thursday at 59.75 million bags, according to a broker.

* Robusta coffee fell 1.6% to $5,655 a metric ton.

SUGAR

* Raw sugar ​​settled up 0.36 cent, or 1.7%, at 21.05 cents per lb - a fresh two-month peak.

* Rabobank said speculators are holding large short positions, meaning any issue in top producer Brazil, such as more dry weather, should be enough to prompt them to buy back their positions.

* Diminishing sugar production prospects in India are meanwhile also driving prices.

* White sugar rose 1.4% at $555.40 a ton.

COCOA

* New York cocoa futures settled down $133, or 1.3%, to $10,267 a ton, while London cocoa fell 1.9% to 8,023 pounds per ton.

* Top producer Ivory Coast will call a meeting with cocoa exporters over the next ten days to prevent them from having to carry an estimated 130,000 tons of main crop contracts into the upcoming mid-crop or the next season, sources said.

* Cocoa stocks held in ICE exchange warehouses have finally started to rise (CC-TOTAL-TOT), dealers noted.

* "Cocoa remains a weather and speculator market. Whilst all eyes are fixated on the upcoming precipitation in West Africa, speculators have been heavily exiting their bullish positions," Rabobank said.

data.title: Equity Markets Fall on Walmart's Weak Outlook
data.description:
US benchmark equity indexes fell ahead of Thursday's close as markets evaluated the latest corporate earnings, including a weak outlook from Walmart .

The Dow Jones Industrial Average dropped 1.1% to 44,133.4, while the Nasdaq Composite fell 0.6% to 19,928.9. The S&P 500 shed 0.5% to 6,111.2. Among sectors, financials posted the steepest decline, while energy led the gainers.

Walmart shares dropped 6.2% after the retail giant issued a full-year earnings outlook below market estimates amid uncertain consumer behavior and geopolitical conditions.

Hasbro shares surged 13% after the toymaker posted a surprise increase in Q4 profit and outlined plans for $1 billion in cost savings.

The 10-year US Treasury yield fell 3.2 basis points to 4.50%, while the two-year rate was little changed at 4.27%.

West Texas Intermediate crude oil was up 0.4% at $72.53 a barrel.

data.title: U.S. Treasury Yields Decline on Favorable Supply Dynamics — Market Talk
data.description:
1555 ET - Two headlines have given traders reassurance about near-term supply dynamics in the Treasury market. The Fed minutes Wednesday brought an indication the central bank is considering pausing balance-sheet runoff until the debt-ceiling fight is resolved, potentially meaning the Fed's asset book may soak up more securities for longer. Then Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told Bloomberg Thursday that the U.S. is still a "long way" from shifting to more long-term debt issuance in auctions, helping stem concerns about another channel of increased supply. The 10-year yield falls to 4.5%, from 4.535% Wednesday, and the 2-year yield declines to 4.26%, from 4.272% Wednesday. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1539 ET - Clorox CEO Linda Rendle says that inflation-weary consumers are still willing to splurge on premium products, so long as they feel they are receiving better value. The company is seeing this trend play out with its trash bags, she says at the Consumer Analyst Group of New York conference. Clorox earlier this year launched a product improvement to its most premium trash bag line, strengthening the top of the bag where rips are most likely to occur. "We're seeing very significant growth from this claim and innovation, with 14% sales growth in our MaxStrength line," Rendle says. The company will test the trend again with the launch of Fresh Step Heavy Duty, a cat litter with a 30-day odor-control claim, which Rendle says is the longest on the market. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1532 ET - Walmart's grocery category remains a "standout category with mid-single digit growth," CEO Douglas McMillon says on a call with analysts, even if it hasn't been able to surpass that category growth rate in the fiscal year. That shows what a "standout" performance is for the retail giant in the current environment. Walmart remains committed to lower prices in the midst of food inflation as merchandise general sales remain a headwind. Walmart doesn't expect its general merchandise category to strengthen anytime soon. Target's 4Q results, expected on March 11, will likely reflect that weakness. Target shares hit a 52-week low in November, after it posted disappointing 3Q results and lowered its profit and sales goals for the year. Walmart and Target trade 6.3% and 2.1% lower, respectively. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1513 ET - Some 55% more U.S. farmers declared bankruptcy in 2024 versus the previous year, although the number is still relatively low versus what was seen in the previous decade. In 2024, 216 farm bankruptcy cases were filed, says the American Farm Bureau Federation in a note, up from 139 bankruptcies in 2023. But despite the big uptick, the total number of bankruptcies is still well below the 599 bankruptcies reported in 2019 — a high point in bankruptcy cases before the disruption of the Covid-19 pandemic. But the trend is worrying, says the AFBF, especially as the USDA forecasts a decrease of crop receipts in 2025. "As the farm economy continues to be squeezed by decreased receipts and increased expenses, farm financials will be strained," says Samantha Ayoub of the AFBF. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1456 ET - Consumers shouldn't bank on BOGO deals for Tide laundry products or Charmin toilet paper anytime soon. Procter & Gamble CFO Andre Schulten says at the Consumer Analyst Group of New York conference that the company will "use promotion to drive incremental visibility of new innovation in store." At the same time, though, P&G will be careful not to reduce the perceived value of its product with heavy discounts. "That doesn't grow markets, it doesn't grow categories and it doesn't grow our share," Schulten says. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1445 ET - Walmart's warning of slower sales gains, together with slowdown comparable sales growth during the holiday quarter, is sparking concerns on Wall Street. The retail giant's 4.6% growth in U.S. comparable sales in the fourth quarter from a 5.3% increase in the third quarter shows lower prices haven't been enough to drive sentiment higher among Americans. "We're growing profit faster than sales," CEO Douglas McMillon says on a call with analysts, noting that Walmart has runway to scale our higher-margin businesses like membership, marketplace and advertising. Its wholesale business, Sam's Club, also saw a sequential deceleration in comparable sales in the period, driving Costco Wholesale shares 2.4% lower. Shares of Amazon, Target and Kroger are down between 1.5% and 2%, while Walmart drops 6.5%. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1435 ET - U.S. farmers are expected to plant more acres of corn, at the expense of soybean, wheat, cotton and grain sorghum, CoBank said. Tanner Ehmke, grains and oilseeds economist with CoBank, said "While farmers tend to stick to historical crop rotations for agronomic reasons and market diversification, corn's price rally relative to other crops suggests a major shift in acreage is in the offing." A report from CoBank's Knowledge Exchange says corn acres are expected to climb 4.2%, to 94.5 million in 2025. "However, the potential for trade disputes with Canada and Mexico could significantly weaken the demand for U.S. corn and curb the increase in planted acreage," CoBank said. CoBank is a cooperative bank serving industries across rural America. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1431 ET - Procter & Gamble CFO Andre Schulten isn't sure the company will hit its fiscal 2025 outlook. The consumer-products company was banking on the second half of its fiscal year to be stronger than the first, but the macro environment continues to be challenging and has yet to fundamentally improve, Schulten says at the Consumer Analyst Group of New York conference. Tariffs, both enacted and proposed, are introducing new layers of volatility, he adds. The company is taking steps to support the low end of its outlook, but "there is some level of probability that external factors may lead to a slightly below guidance range outcome on the bottom line," Schulten says. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1417 ET - St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem says that signs from interest-rate-sensitive sectors make it clear that the Fed's policy stance is still restricting the economy after a percentage point of interest-rate cuts in 2024. Slow activity in the housing sector and other industries that rise and fall with financing costs show that monetary policy remains at least a bit tight, Musalem says. "Low- and moderate-income households, and small businesses are still feeling the effects of interest rates and financing conditions," Musalem says. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1412 ET - Hasbro's licensing business is a huge margin driver that's relatively unexposed to the "tariff drama" spurred by the White House, CEO Chris Cocks says on a call with analysts. The toymaker's licensing unit has grown by 60% in the last three years, making Hasbro the third largest entertainment licensor globally, and the biggest in the digital games space, Cocks says. Hasbro plans to lean into licensing, and particularly into joint ventures and partnerships for its self-published portfolio in the years to come, the CEO says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1344 ET - Hasbro says half of its U.S. toy and game volume originates from China, but the company is working to cut that contribution down to under 40% over the next two years, CFO Gina Goetter says on a call with analysts. The shift comes as the company maneuvers to deal with Trump's new tariffs on Chinese imports and potential levies on imports from Mexico and Canada. Goetter says the company doesn't source from Canada and has minimal sourcing from Mexico. "We're really watching that China rate, that's what we baked in," the CFO says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1327 ET - There are compelling reasons for the Bank of Canada to cut again in March, says National Bank Financial economist Taylor Schleich. Traders have scaled back expectations for a 7th straight rate cut next month, with only one in three believing it will happen. Solid hiring, firmer inflation and less fear about hefty tariffs from President Trump are pushing people toward a March pause. Schleich says slack remains in the economy. As for hiring, Schleich says the job vacancy rate is at a 7-year low and the less timely Canada nonfarm payroll data suggests hiring remains tepid. Finally, Schleich says Trump's tariff threats alone are impactful, citing surveys from BOC and other business groups indicating executives are deferring investment, halting hiring plans, and mulling a move to the US. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

data.title: Transurban Price Target Raised 6.1% to A$14.00/Share by Barrenjoey
Soybean Futures Climb on Short Covering - Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Soybeans for March delivery rose 1.2%, to $10.44 1/4 a bushel, on the Chicago Board of Trade on Thursday, with fund traders covering short positions ahead of next week's Agricultural Outlook Forum from the USDA.
  • Corn for March delivery was unchanged, at $4.97 1/2 a bushel.
  • Wheat for May delivery fell 1%, to $6.00 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Seeing Into the Future: The USDA's Agricultural Outlook Forum will begin a week from now, and grain traders are preparing for the first USDA estimates for 2025 crops. Some analysts believe that the thinner global supply of corn may prompt U.S. farmers to plant more this year, while the favor corn is getting from farmers may push down acreage and output of U.S. soybeans this year. This sentiment helped push a short-covering wave for CBOT soybeans.

A Bigger Pile: Corn fell under pressure to expectations that the USDA may report an uptick in corn acreage, potentially calling for a new record high. U.S. farmers are expected to plant more acres of corn, at the expense of soybean, wheat, cotton and grain sorghum, said agricultural lender CoBank in a note. "While farmers tend to stick to historical crop rotations for agronomic reasons and market diversification, corn's price rally relative to other crops suggests a major shift in acreage is in the offing," said Tanner Ehmke of CoBank. CoBank projects corn acres going as high as 94.5 million in 2025, just below the record set in 2023/24.

INSIGHT

Flat Broke: More U.S. farmers declared bankruptcy in 2024 versus the previous year, although the number is still relatively low versus 2023. The American Farm Bureau Federation said in a note that 216 farm bankruptcy cases were filed in 2024, up from 139 bankruptcies in 2023. But despite the big uptick, the total number of bankruptcies is still less than half of the 599 bankruptcies reported in 2019, a high point in bankruptcy cases before the disruption of the Covid-19 pandemic. The USDA forecasts a decrease of crop receipts in 2025.

Paltry Amounts: Relatively small sales of U.S. soybeans--which have come as Brazilian soybean producers start harvesting and refilling the coffers of the export market--are expected in Friday's weekly export sales report from the USDA. Analysts surveyed by The Wall Street Journal forecast soybean export sales will land anywhere from 100,000 metric tons to 600,000 tons. Last week's combined total was 209,800 tons. But recent data suggest that U.S. soybeans are abundant, and prices are trending lower as a result. In an assessment, AgMarket.net says that soybean basis bids have been trending lower since the start of the year, possibly an indication of more supply availability.

Accumulating Masses: Inventories of ethanol in the U.S. rose through the week ended Feb. 14, said the EIA. In Thursday's report, the EIA said ethanol inventories totaled 26.22 million barrels for the week, up nearly 530,000 barrels from the previous week. It is also above the estimates of analysts surveyed by the Journal this week, with those analysts projecting inventories anywhere from 25.39 million barrels to 26 million barrels. Weekly average production, meanwhile, inched up from the previous week, landing at 1.084 million barrels a day, up 2,000 barrels from last week's report.

AHEAD

  • The USDA will release its weekly export sales report at 8:30 a.m. ET Friday.
  • The USDA will release its monthly Cattle on Feed report at 3 p.m. ET Friday.
  • The CFTC will release its weekly Commitment of Traders report at 3:30 p.m. ET Friday.
  • Stephen Nakrosis contributed to this article.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

data.title: Sector Update: Energy Stocks Rise Late Afternoon
data.description:
Energy stocks rose late Thursday afternoon, with the NYSE Energy Sector Index up 0.7% and the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE) gaining 0.8%.

The Philadelphia Oil Service Sector index added 0.4%, and the Dow Jones US Utilities index was up 0.1%.

US crude oil stocks, including those in the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, rose by 4.6 million barrels in the week ended Friday following a rise of 4.3 million in the previous week. Excluding inventories in the SPR, commercial crude oil stocks rose by 4.6 million after a gain of 4.1 million in the previous week, larger than the 3 million increase expected in a Bloomberg survey.

West Texas Intermediate crude oil rose 0.4% to $72.57 a barrel, while global benchmark Brent rose 0.6% to $76.52 a barrel.

US natural gas stocks fell by 196 billion cubic feet in the week ended Friday, more than the 193 billion fall seen in a Bloomberg survey and following a drop of 100 billion in the previous week.

Henry Hub natural gas futures dropped 2.6% to $4.17 per 1 million BTU.

In corporate news, Diversified Energy shares fell 4.3% after it priced an underwritten public offering of 8.5 million shares at $14.50 apiece in the previous day.

Exxon Mobil and SLB are looking at lithium investment opportunities in Chile, Reuters reported late Wednesday. Exxon shares rose 1.7%, and SLB added 0.7%.

SolarBank shares jumped past 11%. The company said it is collaborating with Viridi to develop a combined 3.06 megawatt DC solar power and 1.2 megawatt-hours battery storage system project in Buffalo, New York.

Cenovus Energy shares fell 2% following its Q4 results.

data.title: Sector Update: Tech Stocks Mixed Late Afternoon
data.description:
Tech stocks were mixed late Thursday afternoon, with the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK) shedding 0.5% and the SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF (XSD) rising 0.1%.

The Philadelphia Semiconductor index declined 0.3%.

In corporate news, CoStar is planning a bid worth 2.7 billion Australian dollars ($1.72 billion) for Australian property listings company Domain, Reuters reported. CoStar shares were falling 1.3%.

Alphabet's YouTube intends to offer a lower-priced "premium lite" service, providing ad-free viewing for podcasts and how-to clips, Bloomberg reported. Alphabet shares were easing 0.3%.

EPAM Systems shares fell 12% after it posted Q1 and 2025 non-GAAP EPS guidance that trailed FactSet analyst estimates.

Unity Software shares surged 29% after it reported a Q4 net loss of $0.30 per diluted share, narrower than the loss of $0.66 a year earlier. Analysts polled by FactSet expected a loss of $0.38.

data.title: 30-Year Treasury Yield Falls to 4.737% — Data Talk
data.description:
The 30-year yield declined 0.027 percentage point to 4.737% today. The price rose 14/32 to 98 7/32.

  • Snaps a two-trading-day streak of rising yields
  • Today's yield is the seventh lowest this year
  • Yield is off 0.249 percentage point from its 52-week high of 4.986% hit Monday, Jan. 13, 2025
  • Yield is up 0.801 percentage point from its 52-week low of 3.936% hit Monday, Sept. 16, 2024
  • Yield is up 0.276 percentage point from 52 weeks ago
  • Yield is up 0.096 percentage point from its 2025 closing low of 4.642% hit Wednesday, Feb. 5, 2025
  • Month-to-date the yield is down 0.075 percentage point
  • Year-to-date the yield is down 0.048 percentage point

Data based on 3 p.m. ET values

Source: Tradeweb FTSE U.S. Treasury Closing Prices

data.title: DANA INC SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Dana Incorporated, a global leader in the design and manufacture of highly engineered driveline, sealing, and thermal-management technologies, has released its 2024 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the current market environment.

Financial Highlights

Net sales: $10,284 million, reflecting a decrease of $271 million from 2023 due to weaker international currencies and declining global construction/mining and agricultural equipment markets.

Gross margin: $876 million, representing 8.5% of net sales, consistent with 2023, despite cost savings and operational efficiencies offset by unfavorable product mix and inflationary costs.

Earnings before interest and income taxes: $226 million, a decrease from $316 million in 2023, impacted by restructuring charges and loss on disposal group previously held for sale.

Net income (loss): $(49) million, compared to a net income of $48 million in 2023, influenced by higher income tax expense and restructuring charges.

Net income (loss) per share available to common stockholders: $(0.39), down from $0.26 in 2023, reflecting the overall decrease in net income.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: Dana Incorporated operates through four main segments: Light Vehicle Drive Systems, Commercial Vehicle Drive and Motion Systems, Off-Highway Drive and Motion Systems, and Power Technologies. Each segment is responsible for its own commercial activities and financial performance globally.

Geographical Performance: In 2024, Dana's sales were distributed with 48% from North American operations and 52% from international operations. The company experienced a 5% increase in North American sales, while sales in Europe decreased by 12% due to weaker construction/mining and agricultural equipment markets.

Sales Units: The Light Vehicle segment saw a 5% increase in sales, driven by a full year of production on a new full-frame light-truck customer program. The Commercial Vehicle segment experienced a 3% decrease in sales, reflecting mixed global markets.

New Product Launches: Dana continues to focus on technological innovation, particularly in powertrain systems, vehicle dynamics, and thermal management, to meet evolving customer needs and address environmental challenges.

Future Outlook: Dana plans to divest its Off-Highway business to focus on core on-highway markets, aiming to improve cost structure and efficiency. The company expects a sales backlog of $650 million for the 2025-2027 period, with $150 million anticipated in 2025.

Operational Initiatives: Dana is leveraging its core operations to improve profitability by enhancing cost structures and gaining efficiencies. The company is also focusing on customer-centric strategies to win new business and capitalize on outsourcing initiatives.

Engineering and R&D: Dana invested $229 million in research and development in 2024, focusing on both internal combustion and electric vehicle technologies, as well as integrating AI and machine learning to enhance product development and performance.

Strategic Initiatives

Strategic Focus: Dana Incorporated is focusing on core on-highway markets and plans to divest its Off-Highway business to accelerate value creation. The company is leveraging its core operations to improve profitability by enhancing cost structures and operational efficiencies. Additionally, Dana is exploring aftermarket growth opportunities and selective acquisitions that align with its strategic goals.

Capital Management: Dana is committed to maintaining a strong balance sheet while balancing growth opportunities with shareholder returns through dividends and share repurchases. The company has a history of paying quarterly dividends since 2012, except for a temporary suspension in 2020. In 2022, Dana repurchased $25 million in common shares. The company has structured its unsecured senior notes to ensure no more than $440 million comes due in any calendar year, with no maturities until 2025. Dana also extended its $1,150 million revolving credit facility to March 2028.

Future Outlook: Dana plans to continue its strategic focus on core markets and improve its cost structure. The company expects to generate free cash flow of $225 million at the midpoint of its 2025 guidance, reflecting higher adjusted EBITDA, lower capital spending, and improved working capital efficiency. Dana is also pursuing the sale of its Off-Highway business, with the expectation that proceeds will be used to repay or redeem outstanding debt. The integration of the Power Technologies segment into the Light Vehicle and Commercial Vehicle segments in 2025 is expected to streamline operations and enhance customer service.

Challenges and Risks

Market Risks: A downturn in the global economy could adversely affect demand for vehicles, impacting Dana's business. Rising interest rates could also dampen economic activity and affect demand for Dana's products.

Operational Risks: The company is vulnerable to supply chain disruptions, including shortages of components and raw materials, which could impact production schedules. Labor stoppages or work slowdowns could disrupt operations.

Regulatory Risks: Changes in international legislative and political conditions, as well as new trade policies and tariffs, could adversely impact Dana's operations. The company is also subject to environmental regulations that could increase operational costs.

Emerging Risks: The transition from internal combustion engine vehicles to electric vehicles presents both opportunities and challenges. Dana anticipates increased competition and the need for significant investment in research and development to maintain its competitive position.

Financial Risks: Currency fluctuations, particularly the strength of the U.S. dollar, could impact Dana's financial results. The company also faces risks related to its pension obligations and potential tax liabilities.

Management Strategies: Management has identified several strategic initiatives to address these challenges, including focusing on core on-highway markets, improving cost structures, and investing in product technology. The company is also pursuing a sale of its Off-Highway business to streamline operations and enhance focus on core markets.

SEC Filing: DANA INC [ DAN ] - 10-K - Feb. 20, 2025

data.title: Trump meets Tiger Woods as part of PGA Tour-LIV Golf talks
data.description:
President Donald Trump met on Thursday with golfing legend Tiger Woods as part of efforts to resolve a schism in professional golf between the PGA Tour and upstart LIV Golf, a person briefed on the talks said.

The person had no further details on the private White House meeting, the second held on the topic this month.

Trump, an avid golfer who owns a string of golf resorts, has become more deeply involved in trying to end the years-long divide in professional golf.

The PGA Tour said on February 6 that after a meeting with Trump it was closer to reaching a deal with the Public Investment Fund, the Saudi backers of LIV Golf.

The U.S.-based circuit had said PGA Tour Commissioner Jay Monahan and Player Director Adam Scott met with Trump on February 4 and asked him to get involved in their talks with Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund.

Woods has been working with Scott and Monahan on the issue but missed the initial meeting because his mother had died recently.

"I think that things are going to heal quickly," Woods said on Sunday during the CBS broadcast of the Genesis Invitational tournament at Torrey Pines, California.

LIV Golf attracted some of the world's top golfers after it was founded in 2021. The best players from both LIV and the PGA Tour play one another only several times a year.

Trump played golf with Woods on February 9 at his club in West Palm Beach, Florida. Trump awarded Woods the presidential Medal of Freedom in 2019.

data.title: Sector Update: Financial Stocks Retreat Late Afternoon
data.description:
Financial stocks were declining in late Thursday afternoon trading, with the NYSE Financial Index shedding 0.9% and the Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF) dropping 1.4%.

The Philadelphia Housing Index was up 0.1% and the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLRE) rose 0.6%.

Bitcoin (BTC/USD) was increasing 2.1% to $98,643, and the yield for 10-year US Treasuries dropped 4 basis points to 4.50%.

In economic news, US initial jobless claims rose to 219,000 during the week ended Feb. 15 from an upwardly revised 214,000 in the prior week, the US Labor Department said Thursday. Expectations were for 215,000 in a survey compiled by Bloomberg.

The Conference Board's measure of leading indicators fell 0.3% in January, below expectations for a 0.1% decrease in a survey compiled by Bloomberg.

In corporate news, Oxford Lane Capital said Thursday it has priced a public offering of $165 million in 7.95% unsecured notes due 2032. Its shares were down 0.3%.

Allstate shares were falling 1.4% after it said Thursday its estimated catastrophe losses for January reached $1.08 billion, or $849 million after tax.

United Bancorp shares rose 0.4% after it said Thursday its board has declared a special dividend of $0.1750 per share.

Lloyds Banking Group shares popped 6% after the company's Q4 revenue came in ahead of analysts' estimates. It also said it plans to implement a share buyback plan of up to 1.7 billion British pounds ($2.14 billion).

(20:51 GMT) QBE Insurance Lifts Dividend as Annual Profit Rises 27%

By Stuart Condie

SYDNEY--QBE Insurance raised its final dividend as the Australian general insurer lifted its annual profit 27% on higher premiums and lower catastrophe costs.

The ASX-listed company, which reports in U.S. dollars, on Friday reported an adjusted net profit for the 12 months through December of $1.73 billion. Profit rose by 31% on a statutory basis.

Revenue rose 4.6% to $21.78 billion. Net insurance revenue rose 7.3% to $17.81 billion.

Gross written premium of $22.40 billion was up 3.0% on-year, in line with guidance.

The average analyst forecast was for gross written premium of $22.35 billion, according to data compiled by Visible Alpha. The expectation was for an adjusted net profit of $1.67 billion from net insurance revenue of $17.45 billion.

The board raised the final dividend to 63 Australian cents, equivalent to about 40 U.S. cents.

Write to Stuart Condie at stuart.condie@wsj.com

data.title: Israel's Netanyahu briefed after reports of explosions on empty buses
data.description:
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu was being briefed on Thursday about explosive device incidents in the Tel Aviv area, his office said, after police said they were investigating reports of explosions on board empty buses near the major city.

The prime minister's military secretary was updating Netanyahu, who would soon hold a security situation assessment, his office said without providing further details.

Israeli police earlier wrote on X that multiple police units were investigating reports of sounds of explosions and a bus fire in a parking depot in Bat Yam, a suburb of Tel Aviv.

The police said so far no injuries had been reported.

Israeli media reported that there had been three explosions on board three buses and other devices had been detected on two other buses, and that the transport minister had ordered all buses and trains be stopped and inspected.

data.title: Citigroup joins corporate retreat from diversity initiatives
data.description:
Citigroup will no longer require a diverse slate of candidates for job interviews, its CEO said in a memo on Thursday, as corporate America contends with a new business climate under President Donald Trump.

The bank will not have "aspirational representation goals" except as required by local law, CEO Jane Fraser said. It will also change the name of the "Diversity, Equity and Inclusion and Talent Management" team to "Talent Management and Engagement".

The news was earlier reported by Bloomberg News and the Financial Times.

data.title: Bishop Street Underwriters Closes Acquisition of Landmark Underwriting
data.description:
NEW YORK and LONDON, Feb. 20, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Bishop Street Underwriters (“Bishop Street”), a RedBird Capital Partners portfolio company, today announced that it has completed its acquisition of Landmark Underwriting (“Landmark”), a specialty-focused managing general agent (“MGA”) based in London. This deal continues Bishop Street’s rapid expansion, growing its investment footprint outside of North America for the first time, and further strengthening the capabilities of its platform. Financial terms of the deal were not disclosed. 

As a well-positioned high-growth MGA with an established panel of rated capacity partners, this deal brings Landmark’s specialized and dynamic team to the Bishop Street platform, in support of building a truly integrated global underwriting business. Landmark’s leadership team will remain intact with Sitki Gelmen as Group CEO, David Ratledge as Group MD and Deepti Janak as Group CFO, facilitating a seamless transition and incorporating the teams’ vision into the future growth of the platform across the UK, Europe and Asia Pacific.

Landmark has served clients in the specialty (re)insurance market since 2017, offering a range of bespoke insurance solutions across various classes including Professional Indemnity, Property, Directors and Officers and General Liability. With the acquisition now complete, Landmark will build upon it’s established market presence and recent expansions into Marine and Political Risk products, with expansion planned across new classes and geographies.

“We’re thrilled to officially welcome Landmark Underwriting to the Bishop Street family,” said Chad Levine, CEO of Bishop Street. “We look forward to leveraging the team’s experience and strong industry relationships to enable the next chapter of international development for our platform. Landmark’s ability to adapt to client needs and attract the best underwriting talent will continue to fuel its growth, positioning the company as a leading MGA of choice in the global market and a complementary fit for the Bishop Street portfolio.”

Sitki Gelmen, Landmark Underwriting Group CEO and Co-Founder, said: “This is an exciting next phase for Landmark. We are focused on bringing specialty underwriting solutions to our partners, and through this partnership, we will amplify our ability to provide leading risk solutions to top broking houses worldwide. The combination of Bishop Street’s resources and our niche expertise will allow us to accelerate growth, expand our product offerings into complementary lines of business and deepen our presence in key markets.”

Mike Zabik, Partner of RedBird Capital, added, “Bishop Street’s growth strategy is predicated on leveraging a multi-jurisdictional footprint. Landmark’s strong presence in London and its expanding global presence are key levers for future growth, both organically and through strategic acquisitions across key international markets."

This addition marks the latest strategic move for Bishop Street, following recent key investments and acquisitions including Ethos Specialty’s Transactional Liability unit, Verve Services, Conifer Insurance Services and Ahoy!, as well as partnerships with companies like Skyward Specialty Insurance and Topsail Re.

About Bishop Street

Bishop Street Underwriters, a RedBird Capital portfolio company, seeks to partner with Managing General Agents (“MGAs”) as well as niche underwriting teams. Bishop Street aims to combine their best-in-class (re)insurance executive team's vision with RedBird’s strong track record, expertise and network in the financial services sector to build a differentiated platform that is uniquely positioned to capitalize on secular growth tailwinds in the industry. For more information, please go to www.bishopstreetuw.com.

About Landmark Underwriting

Landmark Underwriting is a specialist, UK based MGA providing (re)insurance solutions to complex risks globally. Since 2017, Landmark has maintained relationships with all of the significant Insurance Broker markets. From its centre of operations in London, Landmark currently provides risk solutions across Professional Indemnity, General Liability, Directors and Officers, Property and Marine. The company continues to expand its underwriting and operational bandwidth in key territories, driving rapid growth.

About RedBird Capital Partners

RedBird Capital Partners is a private investment firm that builds high-growth companies with strategic capital solutions to founders and entrepreneurs. The firm currently manages $10 billion in assets on behalf of a global group of blue chip institutional and family office investors. Founded in 2014 by Gerry Cardinale, RedBird integrates sophisticated private equity investing with a hands-on business building mandate that focuses on three core industry verticals – Financial Services, Sports and Media & Entertainment. Over his 30-year investment career, Cardinale has partnered with founders and entrepreneurs to build some of the most iconic growth companies in their respective industries. For more information, please go to www.redbirdcap.com.

Media Contacts

Bishop Street 

Dan Gagnier

Gagnier Communications

redbird@gagnierfc.com

646.569.5897

EQUITIES

GLOBAL - Global equity markets fell while gold prices surged on Thursday, with traders marking one month since U.S. President Donald Trump's return to the White House and five years since COVID first rocked world markets.

MSCI's gauge of stocks across the globe EURONEXT:IACWI dropped 0.61% to 881.35.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

NEW YORK - Wall Street slid on Thursday as ongoing tariff jitters and a downbeat forecast from Walmart dampened investor risk appetite.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 572.12 points, or 1.28%, to 44,055.89, the S&P 500 lost 40.99 points, or 0.67%, to 6,103.15 and the Nasdaq Composite lost 141.59 points, or 0.71%, to 19,914.89.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

LONDON - European stocks closed Thursday's session at a one-week low, as investors took inventory of a mixed bag of corporate updates, while they were also on edge ahead of a German election that is likely to change the country's political landscape.

The pan-European STOXX 600 index dipped 0.2%.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

TOKYO - Japan's Nikkei share average fell over 1% to end at a more-than two-week-low on Thursday, as automakers declined due to a stronger yen, while investors assessed the potential impact of U.S. President Donald Trump's latest tariff threat.

The Nikkei fell 1.24% to 38,678.04, its lowest close since Feb. 3, while the broader Topix lost 1.18% at 2,734.6.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

SHANGHAI - China and Hong Kong stocks dropped on Thursday following U.S. President Donald Trump's fresh tariff threats, though losses were limited as analysts said the rally in Chinese stocks, driven by the DeepSeek breakthrough, still has momentum.

China's blue-chip CSI300 Index ended the session down 0.3%, while the Shanghai Composite Index was flat.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

AUSTRALIA - Australian shares are set to end the week on a positive note by opening higher on Friday, likely propelled by commodity stocks on strong underlying commodity prices.

The local share price index futures (YAPcm1) rose 0.3%, a 7.8-point discount to the underlying S&P/ASX 200 index close. The benchmark fell 1.2% on Thursday.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

SEOUL - South Korean shares snapped a seven-session rally on Thursday, as exporters of chips, automobiles and biopharmaceuticals fell amid uncertainty over U.S. tariffs.

The benchmark KOSPI closed down 17.46 points, or 0.65%, at 2,654.06.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

FOREIGN EXCHANGE

NEW YORK - The U.S. dollar dropped against major currencies on Thursday, as investors took a step back and assessed President Donald Trump's latest tariff plans, while the yen rose to 11-week peaks versus the greenback as bets increased for further rate hikes by the Bank of Japan (BOJ).

The dollar earlier slipped after data showed initial claims for state unemployment benefits rose 5,000 to a seasonally adjusted 219,000 for the week ended February 15. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast 215,000 claims for that week.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

SHANGHAI - China's yuan strengthened against the dollar on Thursday, as market sentiment improved after U.S. President Donald Trump said a new trade deal with Beijing was possible.

As of 0331 GMT, the onshore yuan was 0.07% higher at 7.2724 to the dollar.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

AUSTRALIA - The Australian dollar got limited lift on Thursday from a still-strong jobs report that failed to move the dial on the already limited chance of more policy easing soon, while the kiwi dollar weathered another outsized rate cut extremely well.

The Aussie dollar inched 0.1% higher to $0.6350 .

For a full report, click on

- - - -

SEOUL - The South Korean won strengthens against the dollar on Thursday.

The won was quoted at 1,437.9 per dollar on the onshore settlement platform .

For a full report, click on

- - - -

TREASURIES

NEW YORK - U.S. Treasury yields edged lower on Thursday after comments from the top Treasury official eased concerns of any looming increase in the size of longer-dated debt auctions.

The yield on benchmark U.S. 10-year notes US10YT=RR was last down 3.4 basis points on the day at 4.501%. The two-year note US2YT=RR yield fell 0.8 basis point to 4.266%.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

LONDON - Longer-dated euro zone bond yields dipped from around their highest in three weeks on Thursday, while geopolitics remained in focus and markets awaited more clarity around the prospects of increased defence spending in Europe.

Germany's 10-year bond yield (DE10YT=RR), the benchmark for the euro zone bloc, was last down 1 basis point at 2.536%

For a full report, click on

- - - -

TOKYO - Japan's 10-year government bond yield hit a fresh 15-year high on Thursday amid market speculations that the Bank of Japan may raise interest rates faster and higher.

The 10-year JGB yield (JP10YTN=JBTC) rose 1 basis point to 1.44%, its highest level since November 2009.

For a full report, click on

COMMODITIES

GOLD

Gold prices surged to a record high on Thursday, as fears of a global trade war sparked by U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats fueled safe-haven demand for the precious metal.

Spot gold rose 0.1% to $2,936.38 an ounce as of 02:36 p.m. ET (1936 GMT). It hit $2,954.69 earlier in the session — its tenth record high so far this year. U.S. gold futures settled 0.7% higher at $2,956.10. Bullion has gained about 12% so far this year.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

IRON ORE

Iron ore futures gained on Thursday as strong steel consumption data from top consumer China boosted sentiment, shifting investor focus to prospects of growing ore demand and spurring a wave of short covering.

The most-traded May iron ore contract on China's Dalian Commodity Exchange ended daytime trade 2.26% higher at 837 yuan ($115.15) a metric ton.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

BASE METALS

Base metals rose in London on Thursday as worries about the probability of a global trade war were put on pause after U.S. President Donald Trump said a new trade deal with top metals consumer China was possible.

LME copper rose 0.8% to $9,539 a ton.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

OIL

Oil prices settled higher on Thursday, marking a three-day streak of gains, after data showed gasoline and distillate drawdowns in the U.S., while worries about supply disruptions in Russia also supported prices.

Brent futures settled up 44 cents, or 0.58%, at $76.48 a barrel. U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude futures (WTI) for March delivery rose 32 cents, or 0.44%, to $72.57.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

PALM OIL

Malaysian palm oil futures fell on Thursday, as profit-taking and lower palm oil exports estimates until February 20 weighed on the market.

The benchmark palm oil contract for May delivery on the Bursa Malaysia Derivatives Exchange had slid 26 ringgit, or 0.56%, to 4,646 ringgit ($1,049.47) a metric ton at the close.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

RUBBER

Japanese rubber futures strengthened on Thursday as worsening weather in top producer Thailand sparked supply concerns and recovering demand in top consumer China outweighed a stronger yen.

The Osaka Exchange rubber contract for July delivery , ended daytime trade 5.7 yen higher, or 1.54%, at 375 yen ($2.50) per kg.

For a full report, click on

- - - -
